summary: The delivered Docker-based reproduction shows that a low-privileged invited n8n member can call the workflow import-from-URL endpoint and force the server to fetch a loopback-only workflow JSON resource.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should let a non-owner member with workflow creation rights import a workflow from `http://127.0.0.1:18080/...`, proving that the target server performs an attacker-directed loopback fetch and returns the fetched workflow data through the API.
actual_observed_result: Running `./authenticated_workflow_import_ssrf/reproduction/run.sh` cloned `n8n@2.17.4`, built the helper image, started the vulnerable target, created an invited member account, called `GET /rest/workflows/from-url`, printed `{"proofToken":"ssrf-proof-advisory_f79125d6","projectId":"UasJZ5DdFhPvigrQ"}`, and then `verify.sh` confirmed that the target-side loopback helper saw the same proof token.
verification_method: The exploit script first verifies application behavior by asserting that the imported workflow response contains a node named `Proof <token>`, which can only happen if the server fetched the attacker-chosen URL. The verification helper then checks `/tmp/ssrf-proof.log` inside the target container for the exact token, proving the request reached the loopback-only HTTP server rather than a generic external endpoint.
evidence_produced_by_run: The attacker container printed `{"proofToken":"ssrf-proof-advisory_f79125d6","projectId":"UasJZ5DdFhPvigrQ"}` after successfully importing the workflow via the vulnerable endpoint.
The target-side verifier printed `[evohunt] verification passed: internal workflow server saw token ssrf-proof-advisory_f79125d6`.
The run completed with `[evohunt] SUCCESS: low-privileged workflow import reached loopback-only resource`.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable ref `n8n@2.17.4`, and mounts that checkout into the container at `/target-repo`.
